content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 15, 2002

Domingo Ottolia
Planning Commissioner
City of Torrance
1317 Greenwood Avenue
Torrance, California 90503

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I–02–099

Dear Mr. Ottolia:

This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance regarding the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act").¹ Since sending your
request for advice, you have notified our office that you resigned from your office as
planning commissioner for the City of Torrance on April 29, 2002. Therefore, we are
limiting assistance to you to the explanation, in general terms, of the requirements of the
Act. (Reg. 18329(c), copy enclosed.)²

QUESTION

Is there an exception that allows a planning commissioner who is also an architect
to submit architectural drawings for approval to various city departments and have
contact with agency staff regarding the specifics of these drawings?

¹ Government Code sections 81000–91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, sections
18000–18997. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations.

² Informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329 does not provide immunity as set forth in section
83114.

File No. 1-02-099
Page No. 2

ANALYSIS

In general, the Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making
or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest. (Section 87100.) Your question focuses on what would constitute an
"attempt to influence a governmental decision." An official "attempts to influence a
governmental decision" that is before his or her own agency, or an agency controlled by
his or her agency, if "he official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency." (Regulation
18702.3(a).) Although the Act does not prohibit an official's employee or partner from
representing a client before the official's agency (Novak Advice Letter, No. I-97-365),
attempts to influence include the official's appearances or contacts on behalf of a client.

Exception for Architects and Engineers

The Commission however, has recognized a limited exception for public officials
who are architects and engineers. Under this exception, an official is not "attempting to
influence a governmental decision" when preparing drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in connection with a
preceding before any agency. (Regulation 18702.4(b)(4), copy enclosed.) This
exception, which is limited to the preparation of technical documents (Rumansoff Advice
Letter, No. I-94-045), does not permit an official to have any other direct contact with the
agency regarding the client's project except for necessary contact with agency staff
concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared by the
official. "Necessary contact" has been narrowly construed to only allow an official to
respond to questions from agency staff regarding the evaluation of drawings and
submissions prepared by the official or relating to their movement through the approval
process. (Woody Advice Letter, No. I-99-061 and Holbert Advice Letter, No. I-90-080.)
Finally, the exception would not allow the official to contact agency staff with respect to
any drawing or submission prepared by someone other than the official. (Smith Advice
Letter, No. A-96-022.)

The basis for this exception is explained in a staff memorandum submitted to the
Commission in connection with the adoption of the regulation which states:

"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on the professional
activities of planning commissioners and city council members who are
architects and engineers. There was general agreement that planning
commissioner/architects should not actually represent clients before the
planning commission. It was also agreed that such architects and
engineers should be able to prepare the drawings and plans for clients,
even though the drawings and the plans would have to be submitted to the
planning commission. There seemed to be general consensus among the

File No. I-02-099
Page No. 3

city attorneys that this exception is necessary in order to allow the local
architects and engineers to continue to practice their profession when they
act as planning commissioners or council members."

(Staff Memorandum to the Commission, dated April 29, 1985.)

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, you may contact me toll-
free at 866/275-3772.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Adrianne Korchmaros
Political Reform Consultant

sections.question:
Is there an exception that allows a planning commissioner who is also an architect to submit architectural drawings for approval to various city departments and have contact with agency staff regarding the specifics of these drawings?

.
sections.analysis:
In general, the Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making or using their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a financial interest. (Section 87100.) Your question focuses on what would constitute an "attempt to influence a governmental decision." An official "attempts to influence a governmental decision" that is before his or her own agency, or an agency controlled by his or her agency, if "the official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency." (Regulation 18702.3(a).) Although the Act does not prohibit an official's employee or partner from representing a client before the official's agency (Novak Advice Letter, No. I-97-365), attempts to influence include the official's appearances or contacts on behalf of a client.

Exception for Architects and Engineers

The Commission, however, has recognized a limited exception for public officials who are architects and engineers. Under this exception, an official is not "attempting to influence a governmental decision" when preparing drawings or submissions of an architectural, engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding before any agency. (Regulation 18702.4(b)(4), copy enclosed.) This exception, which is limited to the preparation of technical documents (Rumansoff Advice Letter, No. I-94-045), does not permit an official to have any other direct contact with the agency regarding the client's project except for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions prepared by the official. "Necessary contact" has been narrowly construed to only allow an official to respond to questions from agency staff regarding the evaluation of drawings and submissions prepared by the official or relating to their movement through the approval process. (Woody Advice Letter, No. I-99-061 and Holbert Advice Letter, No. I-90-080.) Finally, the exception would not allow the official to contact agency staff with respect to any drawing or submission prepared by someone other than the official. (Smith Advice Letter, No. A-96-022.)

The basis for this exception is explained in a staff memorandum submitted to the Commission in connection with the adoption of the regulation which states:

"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on the professional activities of planning commissioners and city council members who are architects and engineers. There was general agreement that planning commissioner/architects should not actually represent clients before the planning commission. It was also agreed that such architects and engineers should be able to prepare the drawings and plans for clients, even though the drawings and the plans would have to be submitted to the planning commission. There seemed to be general consensus among the

city attorneys that this exception is necessary in order to allow the local architects and engineers to continue to practice their profession when they act as planning commissioners or council members."

(Staff Memorandum to the Commission, dated April 29, 1985.)
